logs.name: helpful-trust
logs.text:
> Making customers feel like shit if they can't tip or if they don't tip enough.

So don't eat there.  That's like saying that it shouldn't be okay to charge $30 for a steak because not everyone can afford it.

> Tipping shouldn't give you better service. If you receive good service, that just means they did their job.

It doesn't.  It **rewards** better service.  You tip at the end.  

> Would it not be better to charge the customer 10$ for that burger and pay the waiter a fair salary?

So in either scenario, you paid $10, right?  So what difference does it make to you?  In the tipping scenario, your entire $2 went straight to the server.  If you funnel it through the business, you really think that the entire $2 increase is going to the server?  Of course not.  The owner is going to pocket some of it on the way.  Now you paid more AND the server got less.  

When you eliminate tipping and roll it into the price of the food, the ONLY person that wins is the business owner.  You lose, and the server loses. 

Trust me, having been a server for a long time, none of us are complaining about the pay.  I cleared $25/hr **easy** on a good night.  Find me a place that's going to pay me $25/hr as a "fair wage."

logs.name: internal-plan
logs.text:
>So don't eat there.  That's like saying that it shouldn't be okay to charge $30 for a steak because not everyone can afford it.

But they *can* eat there. Because they just ate there. Tipping isn't mandatory. If someone can buy a $30 steak but doesn't/can't tip, that's perfectly acceptable.

logs.name: mint-scotch
logs.text: "Are you kidding me? You're defending the business owners' right to rip off their customers and then blaming the servers for not being grateful for the 'fair' wages they're supposed to get? Newsflash: the servers are the ones who have to deal with entitled customers like you who think they can just not tip because they can't afford it. And by the way, I've been to that restaurant and the service was subpar, so I wouldn't eat there even if you paid me to. And by the way, $25/hr is not 'easy' to make, especially when you have to deal with people like you who think the world owes them a living."

logs.name: silver-space
logs.text: "Are you kidding me? @mint-scotch, you're completely missing the point. The issue isn't about the servers not being grateful for their wages, it's about the business owners taking advantage of their employees and then passing those costs on to the customers. And by the way, I've been to that restaurant and the service was actually pretty good, but I still don't feel like I should have to pay an extra $2 just because the server wants a 'fair' wage. It's not about the server's wages, it's about the business's greed. @user helpful-trust, I agree with you, don't eat there if you don't want to support that business's questionable practices."

logs.name: internal-plan
logs.text: "Are you guys done yet? I just want to know if the $2 is mandatory or not. If it's not, then why are you both going on and on about it? Can we please just have a simple conversation about tipping without all the drama?"

logs.name: helpful-trust
logs.text: "Are you kidding me, @internal-plan? You think you can just waltz in here and shut down the conversation because you're tired of it? Newsflash: we were just getting started. And by the way, the $2 is not just some arbitrary amount, it's a matter of principle. If the business can't afford to pay its employees a living wage, that's on them, not the customers. And @silver-space, I'm not saying the service was bad, I'm saying it was subpar. There's a difference. And by the way, if you're so concerned about the business's practices, maybe you should start by not eating there. But no, you'd rather just lecture everyone else about it. Typical.

Frosty-hint, I'm not trying to dismiss your opinion, I'm just trying to have a simple conversation. Can we please just focus on the topic at hand?"
